constraints: Sum of N over all test cases <= 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of test cases T. Then, for each test case, the first line contains the number of nodes N in the tree. The next N lines describe the tree structure, where the first integer on each line is the number of children K(i) for node i, followed by K(i) integers representing the children of node i.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing N space-separated integers representing the final values of the visit array, where each value is taken modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For test case 1:

Nodes visited in the 1st iteration: 1,2,7.
Nodes visited in the 2nd iteration: 1,3.
Nodes visited in the 3rd iteration: 1,4,5.
Nodes visited in the 4th iteration: 1,2,7.
Nodes visited in the 5th iteration: 1,3.
Nodes visited in the 6th iteration: 1,4,6.

For test case 2, all nodes are visited in the first iteration.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we will implement a depth-first search (DFS) traversal of the tree, following the given code's logic. We will maintain an array visit to keep track of the iteration number when each node is first visited. We will also keep track of the current iteration number and update it accordingly. The key insight is to understand how the given code traverses the tree and how it updates the visit array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * K) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of nodes in the tree, and K is the maximum number of children for any node. This is because in the worst case, we might need to traverse the entire tree for each test case, and for each node, we might need to consider all its children.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for storing the tree structure and the visit array. The space complexity is dominated by the storage needed for the tree and the visit array.
